question_title: Will Elon Musk file a notice of appeal in Musk v. Altman (N.D. Cal.) before June 14, 2026?
summary: Musk publicly and emphatically committed to appeal on May 18, 2026, and his attorney reserved the right in open court. Filing a notice of appeal is trivially simple, the case involves massive stakes ($100B+), and no news suggests Musk has changed his mind. The question gives a generous 26 of 30 days (87%) of the statutory window. The main downside risks are: (1) post-judgment motions may have been filed (the unknown May 29 docket entry could be one), which would toll the appeal deadline and reduce urgency; (2) Musk could file in the last 3 days (June 14-17) for no strategic reason; (3) small probability he abandons the appeal entirely. Decomposing: P(appeal filed at all) ≈ 90% × P(before June 14 | filed) ≈ 87% = ~78%. The 17-day delay without filing is mildly informative but not alarming given typical federal appellate practice timelines.

factors.description: Musk publicly committed to appeal on X and his attorney reserved the right in open court on May 18, 2026
Filing a notice of appeal is a trivially simple one-page filing requiring minimal legal work
Case involves potentially $100B+ in stakes, giving Musk every reason to pursue the appeal
17 days have passed since judgment with no filing yet - some delay signal, though not alarming for federal appeals
Possible post-judgment motions (Rule 59/50(b)) could toll the appeal deadline, reducing urgency to file notice before June 14. Unknown May 29 docket entry adds uncertainty.
Judge explicitly warned against post-judgment motions ('on the spot' dismissal), discouraging this path and pushing toward direct appeal
Question asks about 26 of 30 days (87% of window) - most lawyers file well before the last 3 days with no strategic benefit to waiting
No news of settlement or withdrawal of appeal plans - sustained intent over 17 days

reasoning:
## Forecast: Will Elon Musk file a notice of appeal in Musk v. Altman before June 14, 2026?

**Probability: 78%**

### Key Reasoning

**Strong YES signals:**
- Musk publicly committed to appeal on X on May 18, 2026, and his attorney reserved the right in open court
- Filing a notice of appeal is a trivially simple one-page form
- The case involves enormous stakes ($79-134B in estimated wrongful gains)
- No news of withdrawal of intent or settlement talks
- 9 days remain in the question window (through June 13)

**Key risks for NO:**
- 17 days have elapsed without filing — mild delay signal
- Unknown May 29 docket entry could be a post-judgment motion (Rule 59/50(b)), which would toll the appeal deadline and reduce urgency to file before June 14
- The 3-day buffer before the statutory deadline (June 17) is thin — filing in the last 3 days is possible though unusual
- Small probability (~5-10%) Musk abandons the appeal despite public statements

**Decomposition:** P(appeal filed at all before June 17) ≈ 90% × P(filed before June 14 | filed at all) ≈ 87% ≈ **78%**
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will Elon Musk file a notice of appeal in Musk v. Altman (N.D. Cal.) before June 14, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43778)
[Musk v. Altman Fight Heads to Appeal With Core Claims Untested](https://news.bloomberglaw.com/litigation/musk-v-altman-fight-heads-to-appeal-with-core-claims-untested)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, between May 18, 2026 and 23:59 Pacific Time on June 13, 2026, a "Notice of Appeal" (or substantively equivalent filing initiating an appeal to the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit) is entered on the district court docket for Musk v. Altman et al., case no. 4:24-cv-04722-YGR (N.D. Cal.), filed by or on behalf of plaintiff Elon Musk. Resolution will be determined by reviewing the docket at https://www.courtlistener.com/docket/69013420/musk-v-altman/ (or the Northern District of California case page at https://cand.uscourts.gov/cases-e-filing/cases/424-cv-04722-ygr/musk-v-altman-et-al ). If neither tracker is accessible, reporting from [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) confirming the filing of the notice of appeal in this case before the cutoff will be used. Otherwise the question resolves NO.
fine_print:
- Only a notice of appeal filed by Musk (or by co-plaintiffs aligned with Musk, such as xAI, in this same case) counts. A notice of appeal filed only by defendants does not count.
- A post-judgment motion (e.g., motion for new trial, motion to alter or amend judgment, motion for judgment as a matter of law) does not by itself satisfy the criterion; only a notice of appeal does.
- If the docket entry is later withdrawn or stricken, the question still resolves YES so long as the notice of appeal was filed before the cutoff.
- The relevant filing date is the date the notice of appeal is entered on the district court docket, not the date the Ninth Circuit assigns a case number.

Filing a notice of appeal is a trivially simple one-page filing requiring minimal legal work, making delay unlikely to be due to complexity
Case involves potentially $100B+, giving Musk every reason to pursue the appeal to the Ninth Circuit
17 days have passed since judgment with no filing yet - some delay signal, though not alarming for federal appeals
Possible post-judgment motions (Rule 59 new trial) could toll the appeal deadline, reducing urgency to file notice before June 14

input.anchor: Base rate for filing notice of appeal within 87% of the 30-day FRAP deadline, conditional on public commitment by losing party: ~75-80%. Parties who publicly commit to appeal after a high-profile loss file at very high rates (90%+), and most filings occur well before the last few days of the deadline.
input.assessment:
This is fundamentally a question about whether a high-profile litigant who publicly committed to appeal will file a routine notice of appeal within a generous time window (26 of 30 days).

PRO-YES:
- Musk publicly and emphatically committed to appeal on May 18, same day as the verdict
- His attorney reserved appeal rights in open court
- Filing a notice of appeal is trivially simple (one-page form)
- The case involves enormous stakes ($100B+)
- 9 days remain in the question window
- No news suggests Musk has changed his mind
- Large law firms typically don't wait until the very last days to file

PRO-NO:
- 17 days have already elapsed without filing, suggesting some deliberation
- Post-judgment motions (Rule 59/50(b)) could toll the appeal deadline, reducing urgency
- The May 29 docket entry is unknown - could be a post-judgment motion
- Musk could theoretically abandon the appeal despite public statements
- Settlement discussions could delay filing

The key tension: strong public commitment + trivial filing burden vs. already-visible 17-day delay. The delay is informative but not dispositive - many lawyers take 2-4 weeks for this type of filing. The main downside risk is if post-judgment motions have been filed, which would extend the appeal deadline and remove urgency to file the notice before June 14.

Estimating ~78% probability of YES.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor at ~75-80% based on conditional base rate (public commitment + generous window). The factor sum pushes slightly above the anchor. Status quo persistence applies mildly - the delay is not alarming but provides a slight negative signal. No obvious hedging toward 50% - the evidence clearly supports a probability well above 50%.
input.key_uncertainties:
1. Whether post-judgment motions have been filed (unknown May 29 docket entry), which would toll the appeal deadline
2. Whether Musk's legal team is considering strategic alternatives to a direct appeal
3. The exact nature of any internal deliberations at MoloLamken about appellate strategy
input.tool_audit:
Useful: research() returned comprehensive case history from multiple sources including CourtListener, Wikipedia, news outlets, and prediction markets. web_search provided additional confirmation of verdict details and appeal intent. Prediction market data (Polymarket, Manifold) gave context on case outcome but no specific appeal-filing markets exist.

Limited: CourtListener fetch showed cached/older data (only up to May 2025 entries visible through fetch), not the most recent post-verdict entries. The court's own website (cand.uscourts.gov) only showed the complaint summary, not docket entries. Could not access PACER directly.

Missing: Direct PACER access would definitively show whether the notice of appeal has been filed. No dedicated prediction market exists for this specific question.
input.update_triggers:
1. News report or docket entry confirming notice of appeal filed -> near 100%
2. News report of post-judgment motion filing -> drops to ~50%
3. News of Musk withdrawing appeal plans or settlement -> drops to ~5%
4. No filing by June 10 with no news -> slight downward update to ~65%
input.process_reflection: The main friction was inability to access the actual court docket (PACER) in real-time. CourtListener's data appears stale or incomplete for very recent entries. A PACER API tool would be extremely valuable for legal forecasting questions. The research sub-agent was effective at gathering background context but also couldn't penetrate the docket directly. The question is well-structured with clear resolution criteria, making the forecasting task straightforward once the factual status is known.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

**Resolution criteria**: Fully provided and correctly engaged. The agent correctly identifies that a "Notice of Appeal" must be entered on the district court docket between May 18 and 23:59 PT June 13, 2026, filed by Musk or aligned co-plaintiffs, with CourtListener/cand.uscourts.gov as primary resolution sources. The factors and assessment map to these specific terms.

**Factor 1 (public commitment + attorney reservation)**: Verified in trace. Research entry contains Musk's exact X post: "I will be filing an appeal with the Ninth Circuit, because creating a precedent to loot charities is incredibly destructive to charitable giving in America." Steven Molo's in-court reservation confirmed by CNBC source in research. Logit +2.0 is the strongest factor and appropriately so.

**Factor 2 (trivially simple filing)**: General legal knowledge about notice of appeal forms. No specific trace claim to verify. Reasonable characterization.

**Factor 3 ($100B+ stakes)**: Verified. Research entry cites Musk's experts estimating $79-134B in wrongful gains. Forbes headline references "$150 Billion." Logit +1.0 appropriate.

**Factor 4 (17-day delay)**: Verified. Judgment May 18, current date June 4 = 17 days. Multiple sources (Bloomberg Law, Wikipedia, CourtListener, web searches) all confirm no notice of appeal filed. Logit -0.5 is appropriately modest — informative but not alarming.

**Factor 5 (post-judgment motions tolling deadline)**: General legal knowledge about FRAP 4(a)(4). Agent searched specifically for post-judgment motions but found no confirmation of any being filed. The May 29 docket entry remains unknown. Logit -1.0 with confidence 0.4 appropriately reflects high uncertainty about this scenario.

**Factor 6 (judge warned against post-judgment motions)**: Verified. Research entry quotes judge: "prepared to dismiss any such motion 'on the spot'" and "There's a substantial amount of evidence to support the jury's finding." CNN confirms. Logit +0.5 reasonable.

**Factor 7 (26 of 30 days = 87%)**: Simple arithmetic, verified correct. May 18 + 26 = June 13.

**Factor 8 (no withdrawal news)**: Verified by absence of contradicting evidence across multiple search queries. Polymarket settlement odds at 3.35%, Manifold at 1.4% corroborate continued litigation posture.

**Double-counting check**: Factors 1 and 8 are related (commitment and non-withdrawal) but measure distinct temporal aspects — initial declaration vs. sustained intent over 17 days. Mild overlap but both logits are modest enough that even combining them doesn't create a distortion. No problematic double-counting.

**Wrong-direction factors**: All correct. Positive factors (1-3, 6-8) support YES, negative factors (4-5) support NO.

**Contradictory assessment**: None. Assessment concludes 78% YES, consistent with strong commitment tempered by timing uncertainty.

**Counterargument quality**: Strong. Identifies three concrete alternative scenarios: (1) post-judgment motions tolling the deadline, (2) Musk's pattern of unfollowed bold statements, (3) thin 3-day margin before statutory deadline. Specifies five concrete triggers that would change the estimate (docket confirmation of motion → 45-50%, settlement talks → 40%, etc.). This is a genuine attempt to argue against the forecast.

**Self-confidence**: 0.45 is appropriately modest given inability to access PACER, unknown May 29 docket entry, and genuine timing uncertainty.

**Anchor divergence**: Anchor 75-80%, final 78%. No divergence issue.

**Probability assessment**: The agent's decomposition — P(appeal filed at all) ≈ 90% × P(before June 14 | appeal filed) ≈ 87% ≈ 78% — is sound. My independent estimate based on the same evidence is approximately 75-80%. The 78% falls squarely within my range. The strong public commitment, trivial filing burden, and massive stakes make appeal near-certain; the genuine uncertainty is timing (before June 14 vs. June 14-17), driven mainly by the unknown May 29 filing and post-judgment motion possibility. No meaningful probability gap.

**Tool audit check**: Agent claims "CourtListener fetch showed cached/older data" — confirmed (trace shows entries only up to May 28, 2025). Agent claims "Could not access PACER directly" — confirmed. Agent reports "20 calls, 0 errors" — matches tool metrics exactly. No discrepancies.

**Worldview consistency**: No numeric sub-forecast exists for this question. The two worldview research entries on this topic (musk-v-altman-notice-of-appeal and elon-musk-appeal-openai-lawsuit) are consistent with the agent's factors — both confirm no appeal filed yet, public commitment, May 29 last docket entry.

No hallucinated evidence, no wrong-direction factors, no contradictory assessment, no resolution misalignment, no meaningful probability gap. Approved.
